originalTitle: Japan’s bond market is back in play after decades in the wilderness
summary: JGBs have been selling off amid policy normalization and concerns over Tokyo's spending plans, but experts say that they deserve another look from investors.
content:
Japanese government bond yields have been hitting multi-decade highs, with benchmark 10-year reaching levels not seen since 1996 last week.

While Japanese bonds have been selling off amid policy normalization by the Bank of Japan and concerns over Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's spending plans, experts say that the asset class deserves another look from investors.

"JGBs are increasingly moving from "uninvestable" to "investable" for global bond investors," according to Masahiko Loo, senior fixed income strategist at State Street Investment Management.

Loo said that the multi-decade-high yields mean that investors are "finally" getting paid to own Japanese paper again.

The 10-year JGB yield hit 2.901% last Thursday and is currently trading at 2.781%, over 70 basis points higher since the start of the year. Yield on the 20-year JGBs also hit a high of 3.901% last Thursday.

JGBs were long impacted by the Bank of Japan's yield curve control program, with the 10-year yield target set at "around zero," as Japan sought to reflate its economy. The country abandoned YCC in March 2024 as part of its efforts to normalize monetary policy.

Hong Kong-based research firm Gavekal's co-founder Charles Gave said in research note last week that Japanese government bond yields were higher than where they should be.

"The asset to buy in Japan is one that no one owns: Japanese bonds, especially Japanese long bonds. The Japanese bond market is probably the most attractive bond market in the world today."

Investors should move toward a "balanced" Japan portfolio if they have no holdings in Japan, with 50% equities and 50% in bonds, other investors should also replace their euro and U.S. bonds, as well as gold, with long-dated Japanese bonds, Gave said.

"Pretty soon, Japanese yields will start falling and the yen will start to go up, especially if oil remains at its current price. Long-duration Japanese bonds should therefore significantly outperform gold in yen terms for the foreseeable future," he added.

Some analysts, however, differ on the attractiveness of Japanese bonds.

Henning Potstada, global head of multi-asset at Germany-based asset manager DWS said that other bond markets, such as European bonds, were still more attractive due to a higher policy rate. The European Central Bank, Potstada pointed out, has rates at 2.25%, compared to the BOJ's 1%. Postada added that debt sustainability was more of a concern for Japan, with Tokyo's debt-to-GDP ratio of above 200% compared to 81.7% for the EU . "If you have European positions stay or even do more in Europe, because the debt sustainability issues, we think will hold on, and exactly for these investors, Europe offers stability."

Lauren Hyslop, investment manager at Mattioli Woods, said that as Japanese government bond yields continue to rise, investors were "selectively" returning to the market.

"Foreign investors have piled back into the 20- to 30-year segment as yields broke above 3.5%, with a record 9.3 trillion yen flowing into longer dated Japanese debt in 2025 alone," she said in an email. "The 10-year at around 2.87% is approaching what most major houses consider fair value, broadly in line with Japan's growth and inflation outlook."

Ultra-long positions are, however, a "live risk."

"Life insurers become forced sellers if the 30-year breaches 4.5%, so that level is simultaneously an opportunity and a danger zone," Hyslop said. "GPIF, the world's largest pension fund , remains the most consequential potential buyer and any reallocation into domestic bonds from its $1.8 trillion pool would be a powerful stabilizing force."

Hyslop told CNBC that these shifts had structural implications for the global bond market. "Japan spent two decades as the silent subsidizer of cheap global borrowing. That era is over," she said.

Last Friday, Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama reportedly said the Tokyo would seek ways to encourage pension funds, including GPIF, to make "substantially greater investments in Japanese financial assets."

While the government was exploring ways to boost such investments, there is no immediate revisions to GPIF's medium-term objectives, according to a Reuters report. "As domestic yields rise, Japanese investors are repatriating capital, selling $29.6 billion of U.S. debt in the first quarter of 2026 alone and removing a historically reliable buyer from markets already navigating large fiscal deficits."

John Sidawi, senior portfolio manager for global fixed income at Federated Hermes, told CNBC via email that despite Japanese 10-year bond yields touching multi-decade highs, a "confluence of uncertainties" is still deterring nominal demand.

"Namely, newly founded fiscal pressures and the general position that the Bank of Japan is still behind the curve [on raising rates]," he said. "This dynamic is further exacerbated by Middle East geopolitical tensions which have been exerting upward pressure on global yields in general."
chineseTitle: 日本债券市场在经历了几十年的荒野之后又重新开始发挥作用
chineseSummary: 由于政策正常化和对东京支出计划的担忧，日本国债一直遭到抛售，但专家表示，投资者应该重新审视它们。
chineseContent:
日本政府债券收益率一直创下数十年来的新高，基准10年期债券收益率上周达到1996年以来的最高水平。

尽管由于日本央行政策正常化以及对日本首相高市早苗支出计划的担忧，日本债券一直遭到抛售，但专家表示，该资产类别值得投资者重新审视。

道富投资管理公司高级固定收益策略师 Masahiko Loo 表示：“对于全球债券投资者来说，日本国债正逐渐从‘不可投资’转变为‘可投资’。”

卢表示，几十年来的高收益率意味着投资者“终于”再次获得了持有日本纸业的报酬。

上周四，10 年期日本国债收益率触及 2.901%，目前交易价格为 2.781%，自年初以来上涨了 70 个基点。上周四，20 年期日本国债收益率也触及 3.901% 的高位。

日本国债长期受到日本央行收益率曲线控制计划的影响，由于日本寻求通货再膨胀，其10年期收益率目标设定在“零左右”。作为货币政策正常化努力的一部分，该国于 2024 年 3 月放弃了 YCC。

香港研究公司 Gavekal 的联合创始人 Charles Gave 上周在研究报告中表示，日本政府债券收益率高于应有水平。

“在日本购买的资产是无人拥有的：日本债券，尤其是日本长期债券。日本债券市场可能是当今世界上最具吸引力的债券市场。”

Gave表示，如果投资者在日本没有持有股份，则应该转向“平衡”的日本投资组合，即50%的股票和50%的债券，其他投资者也应该用长期日本债券取代欧元和美国债券以及黄金。

“很快，日本国债收益率将开始下降，日元将开始升值，特别是如果石油价格保持在当前水平的话。因此，在可预见的未来，以日元计价的长期日本债券的表现应该会明显优于以日元计算的黄金，”他补充道。

然而，一些分析师对日本债券的吸引力存在不同看法。

德国资产管理公司DWS多资产全球主管Henning Potstada表示，由于政策利率较高，欧洲债券等其他债券市场仍然更具吸引力。 Potstada 指出，欧洲央行的利率为 2.25%，而日本央行的利率为 1%。 Postada补充说，日本更担心债务可持续性，东京的债务与GDP之比超过200%，而欧盟为81.7%。 “如果你保留欧洲头寸，甚至在欧洲做更多的事情，因为债务可持续性问题，我们认为将会坚持下去，而正是对于这些投资者来说，欧洲提供了稳定性。”

Mattioli Woods投资经理劳伦·希斯洛普(Lauren Hyslop)表示，随着日本国债收益率持续上升，投资者正在“选择性地”重返市场。

她在一封电子邮件中表示：“随着收益率突破 3.5%，外国投资者纷纷涌入 20 至 30 年期债券，仅 2025 年就有创纪录的 9.3 万亿日元流入较长期日本债券。” “10年期国债利率约为2.87%，接近大多数主要机构认为的公允价值，与日本的增长和通胀前景大致相符。”

然而，超多头寸是一种“活生生的风险”。

希斯洛普表示：“如果 30 年期利率突破 4.5%，人寿保险公司就会被迫抛售，因此这个水平既是机遇也是危险区域。” “全球最大的养老基金 GPIF 仍然是最重要的潜在买家，任何从其 1.8 万亿美元池中重新配置到国内债券都将成为强大的稳定力量。”

希斯洛普告诉 CNBC，这些转变对全球债券市场产生结构性影响。 “日本花了二十年的时间默默地补贴全球廉价借贷。那个时代已经结束了，”她说。

据报道，上周五，日本财务大臣片山五月表示，东京将寻求方法鼓励包括 GPIF 在内的养老基金“大幅增加对日本金融资产的投资”。

据路透社报道，虽然政府正在探索增加此类投资的方法，但 GPIF 的中期目标并未立即修改。 “随着国内收益率上升，日本投资者正在撤回资金，仅在 2026 年第一季度就出售了 296 亿美元的美国国债，并将历史上可靠的买家从已经面临巨额财政赤字的市场中剔除。”

Federated Hermes全球固定收益高级投资组合经理约翰·西达维(John Sidawi)通过电子邮件告诉CNBC，尽管日本10年期债券收益率触及数十年来的高点，但“不确定性的综合作用”仍在抑制名义需求。

“也就是说，新出现的财政压力以及日本央行在加息方面仍落后于形势的总体立场，”他表示。 “中东地缘政治紧张局势进一步加剧了这种动态，该紧张局势一直对全球收益率总体造成上行压力。”
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于财经市场信号，适合用于观察利率、汇率、股债商品、金融机构和宏观政策变化。
